summary: This second Ghostty devlog covers two major technical improvements: implementing non-native fullscreen on macOS and fixing a subtle font blurriness bug on Linux. The non-native fullscreen feature required a substantial rewrite, moving from SwiftUI to AppKit for window and app lifecycle management while keeping SwiftUI for views. The Linux font blurriness was caused by a subtle floating-point rounding error in window padding calculations that led to fractional pixel values, which the GPU would round up and then downscale, introducing anti-aliasing blur. The post also announces that Ghostty will be free and open source, likely under GPLv3.
key_points: Non-native fullscreen on macOS required replacing SwiftUI app/window lifecycle with AppKit, turning a seemingly simple feature into an 800+ line change
A months-old Linux font blurriness bug was traced to floating-point rounding errors in padding calculations that caused off-by-one pixel mismatches between CPU and GPU rendering
The proper fix involved changing size data structures from floats to integers throughout, only converting to floats at the GPU boundary
Ghostty will be released as free and open source software, with the community leaning toward GPLv3
SwiftUI limitations forced architectural changes, but maintaining native macOS experience remains a priority despite the extra work
money_quotes: If you've been following Ghostty, I very proudly talk about how Ghostty is written in Zig but uses SwiftUI for macOS GUI.
Thorsten was able to delete a file named CursedMenuManager.swift and that's a good hint into the broader reason this was a good change.
I respect that there's a lot of people out there who really like Swift, but I'd much prefer to be working in Zig.
Sit around the campfire friends, because this is a very important software engineering lesson on the insidious nature of float math and float/int conversions.
If it was off by even one pixel, you'd get blurriness. Oh. My. God.
Downscaling introduces anti-aliasing, which by definition blurs edges. Hence, blurry fonts.
So this is also a lesson in using the right data type.
key_insight: Floating-point math in graphics programming can cause subtle rendering bugs that are nearly impossible to reproduce, making integer types the safer choice for screen dimensions and padding values.
